description: Commedia dell'arte – KS3 Drama. Load into Year 8 Drama (or any Year * Drama). Use Lesson Library → Load example pack when on Year 8 Drama and you have the pack.

lesson1.title: Commedia dell'arte – Lesson 1
lesson1.lessonName: Introduction to CDA, audience and improvisation
lesson1.learningOutcome: To introduce Commedia dell'arte (CDA) and its history; for students to understand and show an awareness of audience and the role of improvisation.
lesson1.successCriteria: Understand and play a comedy character more successfully (Pupils target).
lesson1.introduction:
Introduce assessment. Watch factory scene – Chaplin.

Example link: Chaplin factory scene (YouTube)

Q: What worked? Timing, exaggeration, stock characters. Emphasis upon REACTION. The more I laugh with the audience at how funny I think I am, the more they laugh when they see me tripped up by my partner. At all times try to get the audience on your side. EXAGGERATE!
lesson1.mainActivity:
Activity 2: Read Handout on CDA. Note the Stock characters. These are easily recognisable by the audience.

Activity 3: Act out a joke. Remember exaggeration, and reaction. In groups of 4, come up with a series of CLEAN jokes which are acted out. Remember the bigger the better, really go for it! On a scale of one to ten, play at level ten.
lesson1.plenary: Is it clear what they are doing? Are they involving the audience? What have we learnt in today's lesson which we can use in next week's Drama lesson?
lesson1.resources:
• Handout on Commedia dell'arte
  • Example video: Chaplin factory scene (link in Introduction)
lesson1.differentiation: Gifted and Talented: Open questions in discussion; use as model to demonstrate; allocate directorial role; place with less able or with other high achievers; differentiate written tasks; reward via school rewards system.
lesson1.assessment: Playing a comedy character – understand and play a comedy character more successfully.

lesson2.title: Commedia dell'arte – Lesson 2
lesson2.lessonName: Body language when playing a comedy role
lesson2.learningOutcome: Able to understand and use body language when playing a comedic role.
lesson2.successCriteria: Understand and play a comedy character more successfully.
lesson2.introduction: Take the students through the 'Tension States'. 1=beach Catatonic, 7=tragic. Move up and down the scale from one to seven and back and then randomly. Explain that this is the actors craft to be able to play level one with as much energy as level 7, and to be in tune enough to effect the change smoothly. Think of state of tension, walk around and find exact matching number.
lesson2.mainActivity:
Activity 2: Creation of characters from centres of leading. Walk around the room led by foreheads, noses, chests, knees, hips, toes, insides of feet. Spotlight examples – what character is suggested? Mixing centres: head leading person + knee leading person (Master characters usually lead with nose and forehead; servants tend to lead with knees).

Activity 3: 'I'm sorry I cannot' game – in pairs, Master and Servant. Master asks for simple things; Servant gives plausible reasons why he cannot. Spotlight good examples.

Activity 4: Same scene but characters have animal qualities: MASTERS – Chicken, turkey, stork, crab (Pantalone), pig, owl (Dottore), peacock (El Capitano). SERVANTS – Cat, monkey, fox (Harlequin), cockerel, toad (Punch), baby chick, dog (Zanni).
lesson2.plenary:
See some results. Did the animals change the scenes dramatically? Did the animals help us get into role? Which version was the most exaggerated/funny?

H/W: Define a Lazzi routine; how were they used in CDA?
lesson2.differentiation: G&T: Directorial role; more sensitive roles; peer feedback.
lesson2.assessment: Observe use of body language and centres of leading in comedy role.

lesson3.title: Commedia dell'arte – Lesson 3
lesson3.learningOutcome: To be able to use stock characters and improvise a Lazzi routine.
lesson3.successCriteria: Sustained character; suitable for genre; use of pauses, tension, gestures; reference to audience.
lesson3.introduction: Starter: Articulate drama definitions. Go through A & O. H/W: Define a Lazzi (Think-pair-share) – short, well-rehearsed comedic routine. Quick reminder of CDA key features: exaggerated movement, facial expression, masks, gestures, reaction to audience, stock character. Assessment brief (success criteria) on board.
lesson3.mainActivity:
Silly Walks – cross the circle doing a silly walk; whoever you walk to continues. Hand-out CDA flip cards; go through postures and animals. Under lights, walk around masks; find a mask and find a walk that suits it; face as mask.

Lazzi of the statue: In groups of 4/5 – Il Dottore, Isabella, Harlequin, Zanni. Harlequin and Zanni eat stolen food on balcony; Isabella enters followed by Il Dottore who woos her; Harlequin and Zanni play tricks and pick-pocket Il Dottore; all return to statues when Il Dottore turns round. Rehearsal (3 mins), start and finish with freeze frame. Performances and peer evaluation using assessment brief.
lesson3.plenary: Key elements from today: exaggeration, improvisation, role of audience, body tension. The Lazzi will form the basis of your assessment. H/W: Draw a CDA mask for the characters you explored.
lesson3.vocabulary: Lazzi, stock character, Il Dottore, Isabella, Harlequin, Zanni, freeze frame, status.
lesson3.assessment: Peer evaluation using success criteria; Part 1 (group work) and Part 2 (performance) criteria shared.

lesson4.title: Commedia dell'arte – Lesson 4
lesson4.lessonName: Exaggeration through the use of masks
lesson4.learningOutcome: Able to understand and demonstrate exaggeration through the use of masks.
lesson4.successCriteria: Clear character; exaggerated gesture; mask rules followed.
lesson4.introduction: H/W presented. Activity 1: Walk across the room one by one, make walk as unique as possible. One by one, sit on chair then come out and try to make the audience laugh without speaking (1 min per student). Why did we do these? Warm up, encourage comedy acting. Masks require us to exaggerate gestures; used in CDA, depicts character simply. Trestle Masks on floor – spotlight for mood and effect.
lesson4.mainActivity:
Mask rules: 1) Never touch the mask when it's on. 2) Place hair over the mask. 3) Never talk while wearing the mask (unless half mask). 4) Always have your back to the audience when taking mask off or on. 5) Always face forward. 6) Exaggerate your gestures.

Split class: one half watch the other walk around each mask. Stop opposite a mask – what character would have that face? Body? Tension? Which part leads? Swap.

Burglar scene (2 mins): Enter a room at night, creep in through the door. To be performed to music. In groups of 4. Example: Burglar scene example (YouTube). Watch and evaluate.
lesson4.plenary: How did the mask change your movement? Did exaggeration increase?
lesson4.resources:
• Door and table with chair
  • Trestle theatre masks
  • Music for burglar scene
  • Example link: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=dfxv_lw2CDw

lesson5.title: Commedia dell'arte – Lesson 5
lesson5.lessonName: Gesture and movement – Pantalone, Harlequin, El Capitano, Zanni
lesson5.learningOutcome: Able to understand and demonstrate gesture and movement.
lesson5.successCriteria: Four characters clearly differentiated; exaggerated gesture and movement; animal qualities shown.
lesson5.introduction: Meet four characters through gesture and movement.
lesson5.mainActivity:
Pantalone – rich old man, hunches over money purse, slow body, quick hands, tug goatee. Animal: chicken, turkey, stork, crab. Spotlight some.

Harlequin – acrobatic, juggles, tumbles, knees bent, hands on waist, chest out, zig-zag walk, light on feet, knee leading. Animal: cat, monkey, fox. Might steal your purse!

El Capitano – Spanish Captain, chest out, broad shoulders, big steps, leg lifted high, silver sword, feather, medals. HUGE COWARD – kicks feet forward, head back, howls with fear. Peacock then quivering jelly. Spotlight contrast.

Zanni – long nose, arched back, weight on one bent leg, other points out; legs swap bent/straight; sleeps on one leg, head on arm, noisy (belching, snoring); nervous, urgent; limbs 'possessed'; loves to eat and sleep; like a loyal dog. Spotlight.

Activity 6: If time – group of four, one of each character, mimed scene 'The Restaurant'. Servants can't afford pasta! Share and discuss.
lesson5.plenary: Who was really exaggerating? What made each character recognisable?
lesson5.differentiation: G&T: Lead demonstration; more complex character combinations.
lesson5.assessment: Observe sustained character and use of gesture/movement.

lesson6.title: Commedia dell'arte – Lesson 6
lesson6.lessonName: Creating a mask suitable for performance
lesson6.learningOutcome: Able to create a mask suitable for a performance.
lesson6.successCriteria: Exaggerated facial expression; eyebrows; must not impede speech; easily recognisable; reflects character's traits and animal.
lesson6.introduction: Pictionary: draw a mask for Professor, Young lover, Bully, Politician, Policeman. Discuss – exaggerated facial expression, eyebrows, must not impede speech, easily recognisable, reflects character's traits and animal.
lesson6.mainActivity: In groups of 5/6, design a mask for a character. Same group as for CDA performance. Choose from: IL CAPITANO, PANTALONE, ZANNI (Harlequin and other servant(s)), IL DOTTORE. MUST ALLOW FOR VOICE. Examine results.
lesson6.plenary: Share mask designs; link to animal and status.
lesson6.resources: • Paper, pencils, optional: mudroc, water, scissors, rug for mask-making
lesson6.differentiation: G&T: Lead design decisions; articulate character rationale.
lesson6.assessment: Mask design reflects character and CDA conventions.

lesson7.title: Commedia dell'arte – Lesson 7
lesson7.learningOutcome: Able to combine all elements in the portrayal of a character (leading to assessment).
lesson7.successCriteria: Mask rules followed; exaggerated voice and gesture; Columbina and servants scene clear and funny.
lesson7.introduction: Mask rules recap: 1) Never touch the mask when it's on. 2) Place hair over the mask. 3) Never talk while wearing the mask. 4) Back to audience when taking off/on. 5) Face forward. 6) Exaggerate gestures. With masks on – voice and masks. Point out concerns (mask restricting voice).
lesson7.mainActivity:
Columbina and her servants: Columbina (dove/hawk) demands something from head maid with words and large gestures e.g. 'Bring me my hat!'. Request passed down the line with exaggerated mimicry. Fourth servant is most incompetent. Something passed back – 'A cat?' screams Columbina, 'I didn't ask for a cat!' – sneezing, 'I'm allergic to cats... bring me my HAT!' Orders and blame from Columbina; excuses and problems from the other end. Zanni's reaction to audience.

In groups of five: 'Columbina and her servants' scene – exaggerate voice, gesture, facial expression. Share. How successful? Funny? Why? Exaggeration? Gesture? Voice?

Introduction of assessment: Lessons 8–12 Rehearsal and peer assessment. Scenario and success criteria shared.
lesson7.plenary: Key ingredients for CDA performance: character, exaggeration, audience, status.
lesson7.assessment: Part 1 (group work, 15 marks) and Part 2 (performance, 45 marks) criteria introduced.

lesson8.title: Commedia dell'arte – Assessment
lesson8.learningOutcome: In a group of 5/6, present CDA characters with full production elements. Demonstrate confidence, defined space, imaginative props, choreographed moments, audience interaction, knowledge of CDA, sustained and energetic character.
lesson8.successCriteria:
Part 1 (15 marks): Work well in group, committed, reliable, range of terminology, explore genre with enthusiasm.

Part 2 (45 marks): Wide range of dramatic styles, confident on stage, character sustained, role creative and fits genre, strong evidence of group work.
lesson8.introduction: Characters: Pantalone (turkey), Il Dottore (pig), Isabella and Flavio (doves), Harlequin (fox), El Capitano (peacock), Zanni (dog). Full responsibility for set, costume, mask, lights.
lesson8.mainActivity:
Scenario: Pantalone meets Il Dottore. To pay off gambling debts, Pantalone agrees Il Dottore can marry Isabella. Lazzi of the morning routine – Isabella grieves to Harlequin; Zanni and Harlequin try not to watch her undress behind curtain. Zanni and Harlequin deliver letter to Flavio; they teach him to fight (slapstick). Lazzi of the fight. At the bar – El Capitano boasts; Harlequin persuades Flavio to fight him; El Capitano runs off. Flavio tries to scare Il Dottore and Pantalone; then reads his terrible poetry; they agree to let him marry Isabella.

To get an A: confidence on stage; clearly defined space; props used imaginatively; choreographed moments; role of audience (direct address, interaction); detailed knowledge of CDA; characters interact well; character large, energetic, sustained; effective physical skills; creative, suitable, original, energetic; strong commitment to group.
lesson8.plenary: Peer and teacher assessment using criteria. Part 1 + Part 2 = final grade.
lesson8.differentiation: G&T: Directorial role; more sensitive roles; support others.
lesson8.assessment: Part 1: 15 marks (group work). Part 2: 45 marks (performance). Total 60 marks.
